text: In this video, we will be presenting the case of a patient with a recurrent parasophageal hernia who underwent robotic-assisted laparoscopic parasophageal hernia repair with Nissen fundoplication. The patient is a seventy-six-year-old male with a past medical history significant for long-standing and symptomatic GERD, status post laparoscopic parasophageal hernia repair, and stem gastrostomy in two thousand eleven. He had a series of workups beginning in two thousand nineteen that ultimately demonstrated irregularities consistent with Barrett's esophagus and a large type three parasophageal hernia. The patient is taken to the OR for a robotic redo parasophageal hernia repair with Nissen fundoplication.

Four eight-millimeter robotic ports are placed: one superior to the colon, one slightly left and superior to the umbilicus, one between the second and fourth ports, and one ten centimeters to the right of the second port. An air seal port is positioned in the right lower quadrant, and a Nathanson retractor is used for liver retraction. A 30-degree scope provides the camera view, with robotic instruments including a fenestrated bipolar, vessel sealer, and Caudillet distributed across the arms.
The surgeon begins the operation by attempting to establish normal anatomy and tissue planes, focusing on identifying the right crus amidst significant scar tissue from the previous surgery.

The surgeon identifies the right cruce and retracts the fat to the screen right, dissecting a plane between the hernia sac and the right cruce while maintaining the peritoneal lining on the right cruce. The dissection proceeds anteriorly with downward and inferior retraction, entering the space between the hernia sac and the mediastinum. The fibroalveolar plane is visible, confirming correct dissection space. Dissection continues circumferentially and superiorly, mobilizing the shortened esophagus while preserving the vagus nerves. The vessel sealer is used for safe dissection and division of scar tissue, sac, and small vessels. The camera is adjusted to thirty degrees up for better visualization, revealing both the right and left cruce, with the esophagus retracted to screen right.

The surgeon circumferentially dissects the esophagus and passes a one-inch Penrose drain around it for retraction. The short gastric vessels are divided, and the previous stem gastrostomy is visible in the foreground.

The surgeon divides the short gastric and gastric pancreatic tissue to mobilize the fundus for a tension-free fundoplication. The gastric fat pad is dissected off anteriorly and posteriorly, preserving the vagus nerves, creating a window for the fundus to be pulled through. The dissection plane is right on the esophagus, allowing accurate identification of the gastroesophageal junction. After mobilizing the cura, the curl repair begins using zero Ethabon sutures with Pludgets, while a fifty-four French bougie is in place. The first stitch is performed with a needle angled safely away from the aorta.

The surgeon uses a slip knot to tie the knot under tension during the crural closure, with the esophagus retracted using a penrose and robotic arm four, positioned superiorly and to the screen right. Despite a large hiatal defect, only three sutures are typically needed, and mesh placement is rare. The fundoplication begins by marking an area six centimeters distal to the angle of His and two centimeters posterior to the short gastrics with a stitch to approximate the right side of the Nissen wrap. The fundus is pulled through the previously created window during fat pad mobilization and brought through anteriorly.

The surgeon performs a shoe shine maneuver to complete the Nissen wrap around the distal esophagus, proximal to the gastroesophageal junction. A 54 French bougie is in place to prevent a tight wrap and potential post-operative dysphagia. Zero Ethabon sutures are used without Pledgets, with the first stitch connecting stomach to stomach. Additional sutures are placed superiorly and inferiorly, incorporating esophageal muscle fibers to secure the wrap.

The surgeon repositions the fat pad over the suture line. The Nathan center tractor and all ports are removed. The assistant port site is closed using interrupted zero-vitrile sutures, and the remaining incisions are closed with four-oh monocryl sutures.
